items.html:
The duty of candour

When something goes wrong in someone’s care, your duty of care includes being open and honest about it. This is known as the duty of candour. Since 2021 it sits within Care Certificate Standard 3, and for your organisation it is a legal requirement under CQC Regulation 20.

Candour means you do not hide mistakes, play them down, or hope nobody notices. If a person you support is harmed, or could have been harmed, they and the people close to them deserve a truthful account of what happened.

What candour looks like in practice

Being candid is more than an apology, although saying sorry matters. In your day-to-day role it usually means:

  • Tell someone promptly: report the event to your manager as soon as you notice it.
  • Be honest with the person: support your organisation to explain what happened in plain language.
  • Say sorry sincerely: an apology is not an admission that you are a bad worker — it is basic respect.
  • Put things right: take part in learning so the same thing is less likely to happen again.

Covering up an error breaks trust and can cause far more harm than the original mistake.

items.question: A person you support receives the wrong dose of medication but seems unharmed. What does the duty of candour require?

answers.text: Report it promptly and support an open, honest explanation to the person, even though they seem fine.
answers.feedback: Correct. Candour applies to near misses and events that could have caused harm, not only to visible injuries.

answers.text: Say nothing, because no harm was actually done.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Candour covers events that could have caused harm; staying silent breaks trust and your legal duty.

answers.text: Wait to see whether the person notices before deciding what to say.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Being open should not depend on whether the person spots the error themselves.

answers.text: Record it quietly in the notes but avoid telling the family.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A private note is not candour; the person and, where appropriate, those close to them should receive an honest account.

items.html:
Duty of care is not wrapping people in cotton wool

It can feel safest to stop the people you support from doing anything that carries a risk. Yet a life with no risk is often a life with no choice, independence or enjoyment. Your duty of care sits alongside each person’s right to make their own decisions.

Positive risk-taking means working with someone to weigh up the benefits and the hazards of an activity, then supporting them to do it as safely as possible — rather than simply saying no.

Unwise decisions and mental capacity

The Mental Capacity Act 2005 is clear that a person who has capacity is allowed to make what others might see as an unwise decision. Choosing to eat foods a dietitian advised against, or to walk to the shops alone, is their right.

  • Start by assuming capacity: do not treat a choice as unsafe just because you disagree with it.
  • Share information: make sure the person understands the likely risks and benefits.
  • Plan together: agree small steps that reduce danger without removing the choice.
  • Record the decision: note the discussion so the reasoning is clear to others.

items.question: A person with mental capacity may make a decision you consider unwise, and your role is to support them to do it as safely as possible rather than to overrule them.
items.correctMsg: Correct. The Mental Capacity Act protects a capable person’s right to make unwise decisions; your job is to reduce risk, not remove the choice.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Under the Mental Capacity Act, a person with capacity has the right to make unwise decisions, and positive risk-taking supports them to do so safely.

items.html:
Where the working relationship has limits

The relationships you build with the people you support are close and caring, but they are still professional. Professional boundaries are the lines that keep those relationships safe, respectful and focused on the person’s needs rather than your own.

Boundaries protect the person from harm and protect you from situations that could be misread. When a boundary starts to blur, the person’s wellbeing — and your duty of care — is put at risk.

Recognising a conflict of interest

A conflict of interest arises when your personal interests could influence, or appear to influence, the care you give. Handling these openly is part of acting in the person’s best interests.

  • Gifts and money: politely decline personal gifts and never borrow from or lend to the people you support.
  • Wills and finances: step back from decisions about a person’s money or estate, and report if you are asked to be involved.
  • Personal contact: keep your private phone number and social media separate from your work.
  • Speak up early: tell your manager as soon as you notice a possible conflict.

items.question: A person you support offers to leave you money in their will as a thank-you. What is the professional response?

answers.text: Kindly decline, explain that you cannot be involved in their will, and tell your manager.
answers.feedback: Correct. Accepting a bequest is a clear conflict of interest; declining and reporting it protects you both.

answers.text: Accept graciously, as refusing might upset them.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Although refusing needs care, accepting creates a serious conflict of interest and undermines trust.

answers.text: Agree, but ask them to keep it a secret.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Secrecy is a warning sign; boundaries should be open and reported, not hidden.

answers.text: Suggest they leave the money to a colleague instead.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Redirecting the gift still involves staff in the person’s finances and remains a conflict of interest.

items.html:
Helping people be heard

Part of your duty of care is making sure the people you support can raise concerns and know they will be taken seriously. Someone who feels unable to speak up may be left in an unsafe or unhappy situation for far too long.

You can help by listening without judgement, explaining how to make a comment or complaint, and reassuring the person that raising a concern will not lead to worse treatment.

Advocacy and whistleblowing

Some people find it hard to express their views alone. An advocate is an independent person who helps someone understand their rights and speak up for what they want, especially where big decisions are involved.

Whistleblowing is different: it is when you, as a worker, report serious wrongdoing or danger that is being ignored.

  • Know your policy: your workplace has a whistleblowing procedure — find out where it is.
  • Raise it internally first: usually you report concerns to your manager or a named person.
  • You are protected: the law protects workers who raise genuine concerns in the public interest.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the sentences about raising concerns.
items.textField: An *advocate* is an independent person who helps someone speak up for what they want. When a worker reports serious wrongdoing that is being ignored, this is called *whistleblowing*. You should usually raise a concern *internally* with your manager first. The law protects workers who raise genuine concerns in the *public* interest. Listening without *judgement* helps a person feel safe enough to speak.

items.html:
Working through a dilemma step by step

Duty-of-care dilemmas rarely have a single obvious answer. A simple approach helps you stay calm and act well:

  • Pause: notice the competing duties or rights in the situation.
  • Gather facts: what does the person actually want, and what are the real risks?
  • Consider options: look for a way that respects choice and reduces harm.
  • Seek support: talk to your manager rather than deciding alone.
  • Record and review: write down what you did and why.

A worked example

Mr Ahmed has capacity and enjoys a daily walk, but recently he has had two small falls. His daughter asks you to stop him going out. You feel the pull between keeping him safe and respecting his choice.

Rather than banning the walk, you talk with Mr Ahmed about what happened, suggest a walking aid and a check of his footwear, and agree he will carry a phone. You share your notes with your manager. His independence is respected and the risk is reduced — that is duty of care in action.

items.question: Using the step-by-step approach, what is the best first move when you meet a duty-of-care dilemma like Mr Ahmed’s?

answers.text: Pause to identify the competing rights and risks, and find out what the person actually wants.
answers.feedback: Correct. Understanding the person’s wishes and the real risks comes before deciding on any action.

answers.text: Do whatever the family asks, since they know the person best.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Family views matter, but a capable person’s own wishes and rights must come first.

answers.text: Remove the risk completely by stopping the activity.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Removing all risk usually removes choice too; positive risk-taking looks for a safer way to continue.

answers.text: Make a quick decision alone so the person is not left waiting.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Dilemmas are better handled with facts and support from your manager than by rushing a lone decision.
